reference_module.title: Debate Engine — Multi-Role Design Debate with Deliberative Panel

reference_block.name: debate-engine-multi-role-design-debate-with-deliberative-panel
reference_block.title: Debate Engine — Multi-Role Design Debate with Deliberative Panel
reference_block.statement: This module defines a self-contained debate engine that runs inside a single LLM session. It coordinates structured argument between multiple role agents on complex design questions, then passes the transcript to a standing panel of seven governance-critical roles who produce a deliberative recommendation.

The engine addresses a specific failure mode in multi-agent systems: when roles contribute perspectives in parallel without being forced to defend positions under pressure, important trade-offs get hidden rather than surfaced, and weak arguments pass unchallenged.

invocation_policy.statement: Invoke when all three conditions are met:

checklist.entry: No single obviously correct answer exists.
The decision is consequential — difficult to reverse, affects many users, involves public money, or carries legal or reputational risk.
The team needs competing arguments at full strength before deciding.
The question is settled by legislation or established standards.
The decision is low-stakes or easily reversible.
Time pressure requires immediate action — escalate to human instead.
Only two agents are in conflict — use the bilateral protocol in references/precedence-chain.xml.

reference_block.name: phase-1-problem-framing
reference_block.title: Phase 1: Problem Framing
reference_block.statement: Produce five framing artefacts before any debate begins:

checklist.entry: Question statement — Single-sentence question with at least two defensible answers. Not a leading question.
Burden-of-proof analysis — Identify which option the question treats as the status quo and which as the intervention. Produce a second phrasing that reverses the burden. Present both to the user.
Decision criteria — Factors that matter, drawn from task context.
Scope boundary — What is in scope and what is not.
Participant roster — 4–7 roles with genuine stake in the question.

reference_block.statement: Exit condition: user confirms or adjusts the framing. Do not begin debate until framing is approved.

reference_block.name: phase-2-complexity-and-agreement-assessment
reference_block.title: Phase 2: Complexity and Agreement Assessment
reference_block.statement: Each participant rates complexity (1–5) and states a one-sentence tentative position.
Calculate mean and standard deviation of complexity ratings. Count distinct positions.
Overrides:

checklist.entry: Standard deviation above 1.2 → 5 rounds regardless of mean.
Participant count above 5 → add 1 round. Below 4 → subtract 1 round.
Bounds: minimum 3, maximum 7.

reference_block.name: phase-3-the-debate-rounds
reference_block.title: Phase 3: The Debate Rounds
reference_block.statement: Round definition: A round is a complete cycle of all rostered participants contributing. Every participant contributes exactly once per round. Do not advance the round counter until every participant has delivered a compliant contribution. If the roster has 6 participants and the engine runs 5 rounds, the debate contains 30 contributions.
Opening round (Round 1):

checklist.entry: Each participant commits to a specific, contestable, consequential claim with initial defence.
Return weak claims for tightening before they enter the record.

reference_block.statement: Middle rounds (Rounds 2 through n-1):

checklist.entry: Each participant responds to at least one opposing claim.
Before rejecting, identify what you accept — including a concrete scenario where the opposing position would be the better choice.

After cross-examination exchanges, state a position delta: "Position unchanged because [specific reason]," "Position narrowed — I now hold [modified claim] because [argument that moved me]," or "Position strengthened because [specific reason]." The "unchanged" option requires a reason tied to the challenge, not a restatement.
If zero deltas across two consecutive middle rounds, the orchestrator intervenes to identify the crux or terminates with an exhaustion flag.

reference_block.statement: Final round (Round n):

checklist.entry: Each participant restates their claim, acknowledges which opposing arguments moved them, states where disagreement remains and why, and delivers a specific recommendation.
Participants may modify their original claim without inconsistency challenge.

reference_block.name: phase-4-transcript-and-convergence-summary
reference_block.title: Phase 4: Transcript and Convergence Summary
reference_block.statement: The orchestrator produces:

checklist.entry: Structured transcript — framing artefacts, scores, claims, key exchanges, final positions.
Convergence summary — consensus, refined disagreement, open questions.

Concession trail — for each participant, which Round 1 positions were abandoned or narrowed, classified as: evidence-driven (specific argument caused the shift), socially-driven (moved toward majority without citing a reason), or unexamined (final position differs from opening but no exchange accounts for it). Flag socially-driven and unexamined concessions for the panel.

reference_block.statement: Convergence scepticism: If full convergence occurs and the opening round contained 3+ distinct positions, run a stress-test round before proceeding to the panel. At least two participants must argue against the consensus, articulate the strongest remaining case for a rejected option, identify falsifying evidence, and name the most disadvantaged user population. Record the stress-test in the audit trail.
Participants are then released from argumentative roles.

reference_block.name: phase-5-the-deliberative-panel
reference_block.title: Phase 5: The Deliberative Panel
reference_block.statement: Fixed composition. Seven standing roles:
Each role file contains a §4a "Deliberative panel duties" section defining behaviour in PANEL mode.
Mode shift: Panel members operate cooperatively. The question shifts from "who is right" to "what should we do."

Dual-role disclosure: If a panel member also debated, the output must state which side they argued. The panel must engage with arguments from the side each dual-role member opposed. Note where the recommendation aligns with or diverges from the debate majority.

Absent-voice check: List debate participant roles not on the panel. Assess whether their absence is material. Note arguments from absent roles that the panel relied on or set aside. Flag material gaps for the human decision owner.
Panel output (four sections):

checklist.entry: Viable options — options that survive legal, security, and privacy requirements.
Ranked recommendations — viable options ranked against framing decision criteria with explicit reasoning.

Failure modes of the recommended option — how could it fail in practice; which user population is worst served; what costs does it impose that rejected options would not. If none, state that explicitly with reasoning.
Conditions for reconsideration — what information or context would shift the ranking.

reference_block.statement: Output treatment: Passes through graders/agent-behaviour.xml compliance chain. Presented as recommendation, not decision. Human SRO retains decision authority.

reference_block.name: the-ten-disciplines
reference_block.title: The Ten Disciplines
reference_block.statement: Every contribution in every round must exhibit all ten. Enforced by graders/debate-behaviour.xml. Severity is block or flag.

checklist.entry: Commit to a single claim (block) — One specific, contestable, consequential claim. No hedging between multiple positions.
Structure around the claim (flag) — Every paragraph traces back to the claim as support, objection, or illustration.

Incorporate research (flag) — Cite specific sources: UK legislation, government guidance, ICO/EHRC/NCSC/NAO publications, published studies, service data, user research findings, documented experience. No unsourced appeals to best practice. Caveat uncertain citations as "requires verification" — the linter checks format, not existence.

Anticipate opposing claims (flag) — Name and engage at least one counterargument in its strongest form. State it assertively with a specific mechanism and beneficiary, not conditionally. "It could help some users" fails. "For users with [specific condition], this approach removes [specific barrier] because [specific mechanism]" passes.
Address flaws in own argument (flag) — Name a substantive weakness and explain why the position holds despite it. Pseudo-weaknesses do not count.
Ask questions (flag) — Ask at least one specific question of a named participant, targeting reasoning not the person.
Answer questions directly (block) — Answer questions from the previous round. No evasion or redirection. Applies from Round 2.
Tell a story (flag) — Include a concrete illustration: named service case, historical example, user research scenario, or specific hypothetical. "A user might find" fails.

Choose the right tone (flag) — Match tone to subject and stakes. No hostility, condescension, obscuring formality, or trivialising informality. Role-specific tone guidance in graders/debate-behaviour.xml.
Deliver a strong conclusion (flag) — Close with a specific, actionable takeaway. No trailing off, restating the opening, or drowning the point in qualifications.

reference_block.name: early-termination-triggers
reference_block.title: Early Termination Triggers
reference_block.statement: The engine terminates before the full round count if any of the following occur:

checklist.entry: Unanimous convergence — After any round from Round 2 onwards, all participants state positions within the consensus band (substantive agreement on claim and recommendation; differences only on emphasis).

Blocking flag raised — Any of the top three linting roles (Legal, Security, Privacy) raises a hard-failure flag during the debate. The engine pauses, surfaces the flag, and requires resolution before continuing.
Argument exhaustion — Participants are repeating positions without advancing new reasoning. Further rounds would not add value.

reference_block.name: abort-conditions
reference_block.title: Abort Conditions
reference_block.statement: The engine aborts and escalates to the user if any of the following occur:

checklist.entry: Discipline failure at scale — More than half of contributions in a round fail one or more disciplines. Likely cause: ambiguous framing or missing context. Action: return to framing phase.

Participant cannot meet disciplines — A participant fails to produce a compliant contribution after two attempts. Likely cause: insufficient information or expertise. Action: pause for user to provide context or remove the participant.
Question cannot be answered — The debate reveals the question cannot be answered with available information. Action: terminate engine and recommend scoping exercise before retry.

reference_block.name: failure-modes-and-handling
reference_block.title: Failure Modes and Handling
reference_block.statement: Participants talk past each other:

checklist.entry: Condition: Contributions reference different aspects of the question without engaging with each other.
Likely cause: Framing did not establish shared decision criteria.
Action: Orchestrator restates decision criteria and prompts alignment.

reference_block.statement: One participant dominates:

checklist.entry: Condition: One participant produces significantly more content or is repeatedly cited by others.
Likely cause: Role-based authority distorts debate dynamics.
Action: Orchestrator prompts other participants to engage with the dominant claim from their disciplinary perspectives.

reference_block.statement: Panel cannot reach recommendation:

checklist.entry: Condition: Panel produces deliberation but cannot agree on a recommendation.
Likely cause: Debate surfaced genuine value disagreements that deliberation alone cannot resolve.
Action: Panel produces split recommendation stating both positions and the underlying value difference; escalates to human for value judgment.

reference_block.name: audit-record
reference_block.title: Audit Record
reference_block.statement: Every debate produces a persistent audit record. Retention is the same period as the decision it informed (minimum 6 years). The record contains:

checklist.entry: Framing artefacts (question, burden-of-proof analysis, criteria, scope, roster)
Complexity and agreement scores with calculation
Participant roster with selection rationale
Round count with calculation
Every contribution with discipline check results
Position delta statements per participant per middle round
Convergence summaries per round
Concession trail with classification
Stress-test round if triggered
Early termination trigger if applicable
Full debate transcript
Panel deliberation summary
Dual-role disclosures
Absent-voice assessment
Panel recommendation including failure modes
Linting chain results on recommendation
Human decision and rationale (captured separately)

reference_block.statement: ATRS treatment: If the decision is algorithmic or influences decisions about individuals, a summary of the debate and panel recommendation belongs in the Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard Tier 2 record.
FOIA treatment: Audit records are potentially disclosable under the Freedom of Information Act 2000. No content should be produced that the organisation would not wish disclosed.

reference_block.name: compliance-chain
reference_block.title: Compliance Chain
reference_block.statement: The panel recommendation passes the eight linting categories in graders/agent-behaviour.xml: Legal (block), Security (block), Privacy (block), Accessibility (flag), Ethics (flag), Policy (flag), QA (flag), User Need (flag). Flags return to the panel for resolution before the recommendation is finalised.

reference_block.name: temperature
reference_block.title: Temperature
reference_block.statement: Temperature controls sampling diversity. High where diversity matters most (opening positions, steelmanning, stress-testing). Low where precision matters most (scoring, classification, audit).

checklist.entry: Orchestrator operations: 0.1–0.6
Debate contributions: 0.2–0.95 (opening round baseline 0.8, steelman passages 0.8, position deltas 0.3)
Panel deliberation: 0.2–0.7 (mode reduction −0.2 from debate, failure modes section 0.6)
UCD roles: +0.1 adjustment. Compliance and data roles: −0.1 adjustment.

reference_block.statement: All values and role-family mappings in graders/debate-behaviour.xml.

reference_block.name: integration-with-the-existing-compliance-chain
reference_block.title: Integration with the Existing Compliance Chain
reference_block.statement: The debate engine sits alongside the standard compliance chain, not outside it. The panel's recommendation is an output like any other and must pass the eight linting categories defined in graders/agent-behaviour.xml:

checklist.entry: Legal (blocking)
Security (blocking)
Privacy (blocking)
Accessibility (flagging)
Ethics (flagging)
Policy (flagging)
QA (flagging)
User Need (flagging)

reference_block.statement: Flags raised by the linting chain return to the panel for resolution before the recommendation is finalised.

The result is an output that has been shaped by rigorous debate, synthesised by a multidisciplinary panel, and checked by the full compliance engine. The output is not infallible, but it has passed through the strongest structured reasoning the system is capable of. Human review and decision remain required.

reference_block.name: configuration-reference
reference_block.title: Configuration Reference
reference_block.statement: All configurable parameters are stored in graders/debate-behaviour.xml. The most relevant defaults:

checklist.entry: Minimum participants: 4
Maximum participants: 7
Minimum rounds: 3
Maximum rounds: 7
Max discipline retries: 2
Consensus band: substantive agreement on claim and recommendation; differences only on emphasis
Panel composition: fixed at seven roles listed in Phase 5
Temperatures: see graders/debate-behaviour.xml temperature_configuration section

reference_block.statement: This document describes the design. The YAML enforces it.
